title: Rosabelle
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ O listen, listen, ladies gay!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No haughty feat of arms I tell;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Soft is the note, and sad the lay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That mourns the lovely Rosabelle.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Moor, moor the barge, ye gallant crew!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, gentle lady, deign to stay!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rest thee in Castle Ravensheuch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor tempt the stormy firth to-day.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The blackening wave is edged with white;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To inch and rock the sea-mews fly;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The fishers have heard the Water-Sprite,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whose screams forebode that wreck is nigh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Last night the gifted Seer did view
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A wet shroud swathed round lady gay;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then stay thee, Fair, in Ravensheuch;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why cross the gloomy firth to-day?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis not because Lord Lindesayן¿½s heir
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tonight at Roslin leads the ball,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But that my lady-mother there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sits lonely in her castle-hall.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis not because the ring they ride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Lindesay at the ring rides well,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But that my sire the wine will chide
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If ן¿½tis not fillן¿½d by Rosabelle.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½er Roslin all that dreary night
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A wondrous blaze was seen to gleam;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas broader than the watch-fireן¿½s light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And redder than the bright moonbeam.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It glared on Roslinן¿½s castled rock,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It ruddied all the copse-wood glen;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Twas seen from Drydenן¿½s groves of oak,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And seen from cavernן¿½d Hawthornden.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seemן¿½d all on fire that chapel proud
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where Roslinן¿½s chiefs uncoffinן¿½d lie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each Baron, for a sable shroud,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sheathed in his iron panoply.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Seemן¿½d all on fire within, around,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Deep sacristy and altarן¿½s pale;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shone every pillar foliage-bound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And glimmerן¿½d all the dead menן¿½s mail.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blazed battlement and pinnet high,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blazed every rose-carved buttress fair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So still they blaze, when fate is nigh
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lordly line of high Saint Clair.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There are twenty of Roslinן¿½s barons bold
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lie buried within that proud chapelle;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Each one the holy vault doth hold
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the sea holds lovely Rosabelle!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And each Saint Clair was buried there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With candle, with book, and with knell;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the sea-caves rung, and the wild winds sung
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dirge of lovely Rosabelle.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Walter Scott (Sir) delivers a powerful performance in "Rosabelle"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
